era_context: The early 14th century saw the consolidation of the Yuan Dynasty in China, the decline of the Ilkhanate in Persia, the rise of the Ottoman Empire in Anatolia, and the height of Mamluk power in Egypt and the Levant. In Europe, feudalism was evolving into early nation-states; the Papacy was in transition; and the Crusading era was in its final decline. The Indian subcontinent was dominated by the Delhi Sultanate. This was a period of significant cultural production, military consolidation, and the beginning of European maritime expansion.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific and Oceanic regions (minimal contemporary written record)
Central Asian nomadic peoples (limited written sources)

events.title: Arrest and persecution of the Knights Templar
events.description: On Friday, October 13, 1307, King Philip IV of France ordered the simultaneous arrest of Knights Templar throughout France. The Templars, a powerful military-religious order that had accumulated vast wealth and land, were accused of heresy, idol worship, and other crimes. The arrests marked the beginning of the end for the order, which had been a major military and financial power for nearly two centuries. The persecution was driven by Philip IV's desire to seize Templar wealth and eliminate a rival power center. The order was formally suppressed by Pope Clement V at the Council of Vienne in 1312, though the persecution continued for years.
key_figures: Jacques de Molay (Grand Master of the Knights Templar)
events.certainty_note: The date of October 13, 1307, is precisely documented in French royal records. The arrests are among the most well-documented events of the medieval period.

events.title: Clement V elected Pope
events.description: Clement V (Bertrand de Got) was elected Pope in 1305 and crowned in 1306. By 1307, he was consolidating his papacy and establishing his authority. Clement V's election marked a significant shift in papal politics, as he was a French cardinal and would prove more amenable to French royal pressure than his predecessors. His papacy would be characterized by his relationship with Philip IV of France, particularly regarding the Knights Templar and the relocation of the papal seat to Avignon (1309).
events.certainty_note: Clement V's election and early papacy are precisely dated and well-documented in papal and royal archives.

events.title: Osman I founds Ottoman Empire
events.description: Osman I (r. 1299–1323) is traditionally credited with founding the Ottoman Empire in 1299, though the state's consolidation occurred gradually over the early 14th century. By 1307, the Ottoman state was becoming recognized as a significant regional power in northwestern Anatolia. The Ottomans conducted military campaigns against Byzantine territories and neighboring Turkish beyliks, gradually expanding their control. Osman's military success and administrative organization distinguished the Ottomans from other Anatolian Turkish principalities and laid the foundation for Ottoman expansion.
key_figures: Osman I (founder of the Ottoman Empire)
events.certainty_note: Osman I's reign and the Ottoman state's existence are well-documented, though the specific founding date of 1299 is traditional rather than precisely documented. Ottoman chronicles were compiled centuries later, introducing potential anachronisms.

events.title: Chagatai Khanate under Duwa Khan
events.description: Duwa Khan (r. 1282–1307) ruled the Chagatai Khanate, a Mongol successor state in Central Asia. By 1307, Duwa Khan's reign was ending, and the Chagatai Khanate was experiencing factional conflicts and the gradual fragmentation of Mongol unity. The Chagatai Khanate controlled important trade routes and was a significant regional power, but internal conflicts and the rise of local dynasties were weakening central authority. Duwa Khan's death in 1307 marked a transition in Chagatai leadership.
key_figures: Chagatai Khanate emirs and military commanders

sources.name: Chinese chronicles documenting Mongol successor states

events.certainty_note: The Chagatai Khanate's existence and general trajectory are well-documented in Chinese and Islamic sources, though specific events in 1307 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty under Temür Khan
events.description: Emperor Temür Khan (r. 1294–1307) ruled the Yuan Dynasty during this period. His reign was marked by administrative consolidation and cultural development. Temür Khan's death in 1307 marked the end of an era in Yuan governance. He was succeeded by his grandson Ayurbarwada (r. 1311–1320), though there was a brief interregnum. Temür Khan's reign represented a period of relative stability in Yuan governance, though factional conflicts among Mongol nobles were mounting.
key_figures: Yuan court officials and Mongol nobility

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Temür Khan's reign dates and major policies are well-documented in Yuan official histories.

disconfirming_evidence: The arrest of the Knights Templar on October 13, 1307, is sometimes portrayed as a straightforward act of royal power, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complex political and financial motivations behind the persecution. The charges of heresy against the Templars are largely considered fabricated by modern historians, though some scholars argue there may have been heterodox practices within the order. The extent of Templar wealth and power is sometimes exaggerated in popular accounts. Clement V's role in the Templar persecution is sometimes portrayed as passive compliance with Philip IV, but recent scholarship suggests he exercised more agency and attempted to protect some Templars. Osman I's founding of the Ottoman Empire is traditionally dated to 1299, but the specific events of 1307 are less clearly documented, and some scholars argue the Ottoman state's consolidation was more gradual than traditional narratives suggest. Temür Khan's reign is sometimes idealized as a period of stability, but factional conflicts and administrative challenges were significant.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1307 is uneven. European events (the arrest of the Knights Templar) are richly documented in French royal archives, papal records, and Templar trial documents. Islamic and Asian events rely on later chronicles, introducing potential dating errors and interpretive bias. Ottoman sources were compiled centuries after Osman I's reign. Chinese sources (Yuan Shi) were compiled after the Yuan Dynasty's fall. The Templar persecution is one of the most well-documented events of the medieval period, but the surviving sources are heavily biased toward royal and papal perspectives. African, Southeast Asian, and Oceanic regions are severely underrepresented. The bias toward literate, centralized states and dramatic political events reflects preservation patterns rather than historical significance.

graph_edges.note: The arrest of the Knights Templar in 1307 led to their formal suppression at the Council of Vienne in 1312.
Clement V's consolidation of his papacy in 1307 led to the move to Avignon in 1309.
Temür Khan's death in 1307 led to Wuzong's accession in 1308.
